On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Miyakojima]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:History of Miyakojima]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Qq]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:qq]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社[[Category:Pages to be written]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]] [[ja:宮古神社]], Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} [[Category:Pages to be written]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[en:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:Pages to be written]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社[[Category:Pages to be written 2]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[ja:宮古神社]], Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}

Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} [[ja:宮古神社]], Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category: 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category: 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:11]]

On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}} [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}[[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:Pages to be written]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}

Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyako Shrine}}

Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}}

In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}}

{{神社 |名称 = 宮古神社 |画像 = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} |所在地 = 沖縄県宮古島市平良字西里5-1 | 緯度度 = 24 | 緯度分 = 48 | 緯度秒 = 27.9 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 125 |経度分 = 16 | 経度秒 = 49.5 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |祭神 = 速玉男尊
伊弉冉尊
事解男尊
目黒盛定政命
與那覇恵源命
仲宗根玄雅命 |社格 = |創建 = [[天正]]18年（[[1590年]]） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[10月26日]] |神事 = }} '''宮古神社'''（みやこじんじゃ）は、[[沖縄県]][[宮古島市]]に鎮座する[[神社]]である。日本最南端の神社である[http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html 宮古神社について] 宮古神社。 == 祭神 == * [[熊野権現|熊野三神]] ** [[イザナミ|伊弉冉尊]] ** 速玉男尊 ** 事解男尊 * 豊見親三神 ** 目黒盛定政命（[[目黒盛豊見親]]） ** 與那覇恵源命（[[与那覇勢頭豊見親]]） ** 仲宗根玄雅命（[[仲宗根豊見親]]） 豊見親三神は、かつて[[宮古島]]を統治した3人の豊見親（首長の尊称）を神としたものである。 == 由緒 == 由来記などによると、[[1590年]]（天正18年）、平良大首里大屋子が[[首里城]]へ貢納品を納めた帰路に海難に遭って漂流し[[李氏朝鮮|朝鮮]]へ漂着した。朝鮮では異国の賊と見做されて斬られそうになったが、故国の神に祈り、地面に「琉球」と書いたところ、漸く琉球からの漂着民であることが認められて5年間手厚く保護され、更に[[北京]]に送られて3年後に、琉球からの進貢船に乗って8年後に無事帰島することができた。このことを神のおかげとして、[[沖縄本島]]の[[波上宮]]より熊野三神を勧請し、宮古権現堂を建立した[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ 赤瓦屋根がお目見え/新宮古神社が完成 25日に正遷座祭] 宮古毎日新聞、2010年6月10日。[[1611年]]（慶長16年）、[[薩摩藩]]による[[先島諸島|先島]]検地の際、上奏により瓦葺に改築[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en 宮古島旧記] 1707年頃成立 {{404|date=2024-03}}。以後、[[琉球処分]]に至るまで公費をもって管理された。 [[1925年]]（[[大正]]14年）に豊見親二柱（與那覇恵源命（与那覇勢頭豊見親）及び仲宗根玄雅命（仲宗根豊見親））を祭る町社・宮古神社が創建された宮古神社平成御造営竣工記念誌 2011年。 [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）に紀元二千六百年記念事業として、当時荒廃していた両社を合わせた新・宮古神社の建立が決定し、県社への昇格を目指し奉賛会を組織。[[1941年]]（昭和16年）、宮古権現堂を宮古神社に改称し、町社・宮古神社の二柱の神を増祀。境内地を西里5番地（現在の境内地）に移し、[[1944年]]（昭和19年）に新・宮古神社へ遷座が行われた。県社昇格が内定したが、戦災炎上。[[漲水御嶽]]に祭神を一時遷祀。[[1956年]]（昭和31年）、目黒盛定政命（目黒盛豊見親）を増祀。[[1980年]]（昭和55年）、旧境内地（西里1番地）に本殿、拝殿を再興し、遷座祭を斎行。 社殿の老朽化、宮古島市の誕生などの節目を迎え、[[2008年]]（[[平成]]19年）、「宮古神社御造営奉賛会」を組織。平成の御造営に向けて活動を開始する。[[2010年]]（平成22年）に、かつての境内地である西里5番地に新社殿が竣工、[[6月25日]]に遷座祭を斎行した[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ 宮古神社 厳かに遷座祭/たいまつともし、神移す] 宮古毎日新聞、2010年6月26日。 == 社殿 == 本殿は[[沖縄赤瓦|赤瓦]]葺き。本殿及び[[鳥居|二の鳥居]]には、[[ラオス]]産の千年木が用いられている。本殿の両脇には、[[鉄筋コンクリート構造|鉄筋コンクリート造]]平屋赤瓦葺きの社務所及び休憩所が設けられている。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|宮古神社}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html 宮古神社]（公式サイト） == 関連項目 == * [[琉球神道]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやこしんしや}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]]

與那覇恵源命
仲宗根玄雅命 |社格 = |創建 = [[天正]]18年（[[1590年]]） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[10月26日]] |神事 = }} '''宮古神社'''（みやこじんじゃ）は、[[沖縄県]][[宮古島市]]に鎮座する[[神社]]である。日本最南端の神社である[http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html 宮古神社について] 宮古神社。 == 祭神 == * [[熊野権現|熊野三神]] ** [[イザナミ|伊弉冉尊]] ** 速玉男尊 ** 事解男尊 * 豊見親三神 ** 目黒盛定政命（[[目黒盛豊見親]]） ** 與那覇恵源命（[[与那覇勢頭豊見親]]） ** 仲宗根玄雅命（[[仲宗根豊見親]]） 豊見親三神は、かつて[[宮古島]]を統治した3人の豊見親（首長の尊称）を神としたものである。 == 由緒 == 由来記などによると、[[1590年]]（天正18年）、平良大首里大屋子が[[首里城]]へ貢納品を納めた帰路に海難に遭って漂流し[[李氏朝鮮|朝鮮]]へ漂着した。朝鮮では異国の賊と見做されて斬られそうになったが、故国の神に祈り、地面に「琉球」と書いたところ、漸く琉球からの漂着民であることが認められて5年間手厚く保護され、更に[[北京]]に送られて3年後に、琉球からの進貢船に乗って8年後に無事帰島することができた。このことを神のおかげとして、[[沖縄本島]]の[[波上宮]]より熊野三神を勧請し、宮古権現堂を建立した[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ 赤瓦屋根がお目見え/新宮古神社が完成 25日に正遷座祭] 宮古毎日新聞、2010年6月10日。[[1611年]]（慶長16年）、[[薩摩藩]]による[[先島諸島|先島]]検地の際、上奏により瓦葺に改築[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en 宮古島旧記] 1707年頃成立 {{404|date=2024-03}}。以後、[[琉球処分]]に至るまで公費をもって管理された。 [[1925年]]（[[大正]]14年）に豊見親二柱（與那覇恵源命（与那覇勢頭豊見親）及び仲宗根玄雅命（仲宗根豊見親））を祭る町社・宮古神社が創建された宮古神社平成御造営竣工記念誌 2011年。 [[1940年]]（[[昭和]]15年）に紀元二千六百年記念事業として、当時荒廃していた両社を合わせた新・宮古神社の建立が決定し、県社への昇格を目指し奉賛会を組織。[[1941年]]（昭和16年）、宮古権現堂を宮古神社に改称し、町社・宮古神社の二柱の神を増祀。境内地を西里5番地（現在の境内地）に移し、[[1944年]]（昭和19年）に新・宮古神社へ遷座が行われた。県社昇格が内定したが、戦災炎上。[[漲水御嶽]]に祭神を一時遷祀。[[1956年]]（昭和31年）、目黒盛定政命（目黒盛豊見親）を増祀。[[1980年]]（昭和55年）、旧境内地（西里1番地）に本殿、拝殿を再興し、遷座祭を斎行。 社殿の老朽化、宮古島市の誕生などの節目を迎え、[[2008年]]（[[平成]]19年）、「宮古神社御造営奉賛会」を組織。平成の御造営に向けて活動を開始する。[[2010年]]（平成22年）に、かつての境内地である西里5番地に新社殿が竣工、[[6月25日]]に遷座祭を斎行した[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ 宮古神社 厳かに遷座祭/たいまつともし、神移す] 宮古毎日新聞、2010年6月26日。 == 社殿 == 本殿は[[沖縄赤瓦|赤瓦]]葺き。本殿及び[[鳥居|二の鳥居]]には、[[ラオス]]産の千年木が用いられている。本殿の両脇には、[[鉄筋コンクリート構造|鉄筋コンクリート造]]平屋赤瓦葺きの社務所及び休憩所が設けられている。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|宮古神社}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html 宮古神社]（公式サイト） == 関連項目 == * [[琉球神道]] {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやこしんしや}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyako Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}}

They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyako Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]]

In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]]\n{{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]]

In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:translated pages without meta categories]]\n{{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]]

On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]]

On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[en:Miyako Shrine]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]]

The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}
