Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Miyakojima]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[Naminoue Shrine]] in [[Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島の Municipal History]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島の Municipal History]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = [[File:Miyakojinjya.jpg|280px]] {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280}} | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:宮古島の Municipal History]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}}

Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|qid=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[sn:Miyako Shrine]] [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}}

On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}}

revision.text:
[[sn:commons:Category:Miyako-jinja]][[ja:宮古神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11453485]] Wikidata name: Miyako Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = File:Miyakojinjya.jpg | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Miyako-jinja]][[ja:宮古神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11453485]] Wikidata name: Miyako Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = File:Miyakojinjya.jpg | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11453485}}

Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] }} [[ja:宮古神社]] [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11453485}}

Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:宮古神社]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** [[:en:Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]] ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled [[:en:Miyako Island|Miyako Island]][http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in [[:en:1590|1590]] (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to [[:en:Shuri Castle|Shuri Castle]], encountered a shipwreck and drifted to [[:en:Joseon|Joseon]] (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to [[:en:Beijing|Beijing]] and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in [[:en:Okinawa Island|Okinawa Island]], and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In [[:en:1611|1611]] (Keichō 16), during a survey by the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma Domain]], the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]], the shrine was maintained with public funds. In [[:en:1925|1925]] (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In [[:en:1940|1940]] (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In [[:en:1941|1941]] (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in [[:en:1944|1944]] (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In [[:en:1956|1956]] (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In [[:en:1980|1980]] (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in [[:en:2008|2008]] (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In [[:en:2010|2010]] (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on [[:en:June 25|June 25]][http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宮古神社]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to {{ill|Beijing|en|Beijing|WD=Q956|lt=Beijing}} and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宮古神社]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Miyako-jinja]][[ja:宮古神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Miyako-jinja]][[ja:宮古神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Miyako-jinja]][[ja:宮古神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyako Shrine | image = File:Miyakojinjya.jpg | location = 5-1 Nishizato, Hirara, [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan | type degrees = 24 | type minutes = 48 | type seconds = 27.9 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 125 | longitude minutes = 16 | longitude seconds = 49.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | deity = Hayatama-no-Mikoto
Izanami-no-Mikoto
Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto
Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto
Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto
Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island}}[http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in {{ill|1590|en|1590|WD=Q6764|lt=1590}} (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle}}, encountered a shipwreck and drifted to {{ill|Joseon|en|Joseon|WD=Q28179|lt=Joseon}} (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to {{ill|Beijing|en|Beijing|WD=Q956|lt=Beijing}} and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine}} in {{ill|Okinawa Island|en|Okinawa Island|WD=Q600614|lt=Okinawa Island}}, and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In {{ill|1611|en|1611|WD=Q6768|lt=1611}} (Keichō 16), during a survey by the {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|WD=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain}}, the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|WD=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition}}, the shrine was maintained with public funds. In {{ill|1925|en|1925|WD=Q18107|lt=1925}} (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In {{ill|1940|en|1940|WD=Q18633|lt=1940}} (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In {{ill|1941|en|1941|WD=Q5231|lt=1941}} (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in {{ill|1944|en|1944|WD=Q5268|lt=1944}} (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956}} (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980}} (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in {{ill|2008|en|2008|WD=Q2004|lt=2008}} (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In {{ill|2010|en|2010|WD=Q1995|lt=2010}} (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on {{ill|June 25|en|June 25|WD=Q2662|lt=June 25}}[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宮古神社]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto | Shrine Rank = | founded = [[Tenshō]] 18 (1590) | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = October 26 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Miyakojima|WD=Q714636|1=Miyakojima, Okinawa}} | coordinates = {{Coord|24.807767|N|125.280436|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Miyako Shrine''' (宮古神社 , Miyako-jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Miyakojima|Miyakojima]], {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. == Deities == * [[Kumano Gongen|Kumano Sanshin]] ** {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} ** Hayatama-no-Mikoto ** Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto * Toyomioya Sanshin ** Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto (Megurosoku Toyomioya) ** Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto (Yonaha Seitō Toyomioya) ** Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto (Nakasone Toyomioya) The Toyomioya Sanshin are three chiefs who once ruled {{ill|Miyako Island|en|Miyako Island|WD=Q700946|lt=Miyako Island|1=Miyako Island}}[http://www.miyako-jinja.com/about.html About Miyako Shrine] Miyako Shrine. == History == According to historical records, in {{ill|1590|en|1590|WD=Q6764|lt=1590|1=1590}} (Tenshō 18), Hirara Ōshiro Ōyako, after delivering tribute goods to {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|WD=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}}, encountered a shipwreck and drifted to {{ill|Joseon|en|Joseon|WD=Q28179|lt=Joseon|1=Joseon}} (Korea). Initially considered foreign invaders, they were about to be executed, but after praying to the gods and writing "Ryukyu" on the ground, they were recognized as Ryukyuans and were given generous protection for five years. They were then sent to {{ill|Beijing|en|Beijing|WD=Q956|lt=Beijing|1=Beijing}} and, three years later, safely returned to Miyako Island after eight years. To express gratitude to the gods, the [[Kumano Sanshin]] were enshrined from {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|WD=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} in {{ill|Okinawa Island|en|Okinawa Island|WD=Q600614|lt=Okinawa Island|1=Okinawa Island}}, and Miyako Gongen-dō was established[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4488/ Red Tile Roof Unveiled/New Miyako Shrine Completed; Re-dedication ceremony on 25th] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 10, 2010. In {{ill|1611|en|1611|WD=Q6768|lt=1611|1=1611}} (Keichō 16), during a survey by the {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|WD=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}}, the shrine was rebuilt with tile roofing following a report[http://jairo.nii.ac.jp/0048/00004207/en Miyakojima Old Records] established around 1707. From then until the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|WD=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}}, the shrine was maintained with public funds. In {{ill|1925|en|1925|WD=Q18107|lt=1925|1=1925}} (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In {{ill|1940|en|1940|WD=Q18633|lt=1940|1=1940}} (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In {{ill|1941|en|1941|WD=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}} (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in {{ill|1944|en|1944|WD=Q5268|lt=1944|1=1944}} (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}} (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}} (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in {{ill|2008|en|2008|WD=Q2004|lt=2008|1=2008}} (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In {{ill|2010|en|2010|WD=Q1995|lt=2010|1=2010}} (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on {{ill|June 25|en|June 25|WD=Q2662|lt=June 25|1=June 25}}[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宮古神社]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

From then until the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|WD=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}}, the shrine was maintained with public funds. From then until the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|WD=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}}, the shrine was maintained with public funds. In {{ill|1925|en|1925|WD=Q18107|lt=1925|1=1925}} (Taishō 14), a town shrine, Miyako Shrine, was established to enshrine two additional Toyomioya deities (Yonaha Keigen-no-Mikoto and Nakasone Genka-no-Mikoto)Miyako Shrine Heisei Rebuilding Commemorative Book, 2011. In {{ill|1940|en|1940|WD=Q18633|lt=1940|1=1940}} (Shōwa 15), as part of the 2600th anniversary of the Japanese imperial era, the deteriorated shrines were merged, and a new Miyako Shrine was established with the aim of being promoted to a prefectural shrine. In {{ill|1941|en|1941|WD=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}} (Shōwa 16), Miyako Gongen-dō was renamed Miyako Shrine, and the two Toyomioya deities were added. The precinct was moved to Nishizato 5 (current location), and in {{ill|1944|en|1944|WD=Q5268|lt=1944|1=1944}} (Shōwa 19), the new Miyako Shrine was completed. However, it was destroyed by war. The deities were temporarily enshrined at [[Hirara Mangrove]]. In {{ill|1956|en|1956|WD=Q5221|lt=1956|1=1956}} (Shōwa 31), Megurosoku Sadamasa-no-Mikoto was added. In {{ill|1980|en|1980|WD=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}} (Shōwa 55), the main hall and worship hall were rebuilt at the former site (Nishizato 1), and a relocation ceremony was held. Due to the aging of the shrine and the establishment of Miyakojima City, the "Miyako Shrine Rebuilding Committee" was formed in {{ill|2008|en|2008|WD=Q2004|lt=2008|1=2008}} (Heisei 19), and rebuilding activities began. In {{ill|2010|en|2010|WD=Q1995|lt=2010|1=2010}} (Heisei 22), a new shrine building was completed at the former site (Nishizato 5), and a relocation ceremony was held on {{ill|June 25|en|June 25|WD=Q2662|lt=June 25|1=June 25}}[http://www.miyakomainichi.com/2010/06/4224/ Miyako Shrine Solemn Relocation ceremony/Torches Lit, Gods Moved] Miyako Mainichi Newspaper, June 26, 2010. == Shrine Buildings == The main hall has a [[red tile]] roof. The main hall and the second torii gate are made from thousand-year-old Laotian wood. On both sides of the main hall are single-story red tile-roofed office buildings and resting areas made of reinforced concrete. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Miyako-jinja|Miyako Shrine}} * [http://www.miyako-jinja.com/index.html Miyako Shrine] (official website) == Related Pages == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|宮古神社|version=103004796|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:宮古神社]] {{Commons category|Miyako-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11453485}} [[Category:Architecture of Miyakojima City]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Municipal History of Miyakojima]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
